explicit_text: I was living in Chicago when I entered the service in April 1944. I grew up on the Northside, around Milwaukee, Armitage, and Western, at 2440 West Courtland Street. I went to Lane Tech High School there and had a good life playing sports. Chicago was home to me and my family before I was drafted into the Army.

That city was home to me and my family before I was drafted into the Army.

explicit_text: I attended Lane Tech High School in Chicago and graduated in January 1944. I loved it there, especially playing basketball, football, and baseball. The teachers and athletic coaches were wonderful, and it was a big part of my youth before joining the Army. Many of my friends from Lane Tech went to Europe during the war.

explicit_text: My brother Walter worked on the lenses for the Norden Bombsight during the war, which was considered important military work. Because of that, he was deferred from joining the Army. I remember that his work on the bombsight lenses was critical and kept him from being drafted.

explicit_text: My parents came from Poland, and it was very personal for me during the war. My Uncle Andrew was killed by the Nazis on his farm there, which deeply affected my family. My mother cried over the loss, and that was part of why I wanted to join the Army and fight in Europe.

explicit_text: Nazi Germany was the enemy that took my uncle’s life in Poland. I wanted to join the Army early to fight against them in Europe, but I ended up being sent to the Pacific instead. The Nazis and their SS were responsible for many atrocities that affected my family personally.

explicit_text: I was drafted into the United States Army in April 1944 after finishing high school. I trained at Fort Sheridan and Fort Riley before being sent overseas. The Army gave me food, clothing, and tough training, including working with horses and mules in the 124th Cavalry Regiment. I served in the Pacific Theater during the war.

explicit_text: My brother Stanley joined the Coast Guard during the war. While I was drafted into the Army, he served in the Coast Guard, so we were both contributing to the war effort in different branches. It was a family effort to support the country.

explicit_text: I didn’t serve in the United States Marine Corps, but when I was drafted, I didn’t have a preference for any branch. I was willing to go wherever they needed me, whether it was the Marines, Coast Guard, Army Air Corps, or Army. Ultimately, I was placed in the Army.

explicit_text: I was born during the Great Depression, and I remember how hard times were. My mother had to put cardboard in my shoes because the leather wore out. People in our neighborhood lost jobs and homes, and some even committed suicide. It was a tough childhood before the war.
